papers.title: TempCompass: Do Video LLMs Really Understand Videos?
papers.quick_takeaway: A clean diagnostic benchmark for whether a model really uses temporal evidence instead of static frame cues.
papers.problem: Video evaluation often hides temporal failure behind single-frame shortcuts and narrow QA formats.
papers.method_or_setup: Uses conflicting video pairs, multiple temporal aspect categories, diverse instructions, and LLM-based evaluation.
papers.why_it_matters: If a model fails here, later gains on grounding or reasoning may just be benchmark luck.
key_points: Conflicting videos reduce easy static shortcuts.
Multiple temporal aspects expose different failure modes.
Current Video LLMs and even Image LLMs still perform poorly on temporal perception.
next_after_summary: Compare against VidHalluc to separate perception failure from hallucination failure.
Check which temporal aspect breaks first.
Use it as the first diagnostic lens before reading method papers.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: Video-MME: The First-Ever Comprehensive Evaluation Benchmark of Multi-modal LLMs in Video Analysis
papers.quick_takeaway: A field-standard benchmark for broad video analysis that is valuable because it is comprehensive, but less clean than TempCompass for isolating temporal evidence use.
papers.problem: The field lacked a comprehensive, carefully annotated benchmark for multimodal video analysis across domains, durations, and modality settings.
papers.method_or_setup: Builds a benchmark with 900 manually selected videos, 2,700 QA pairs, multiple duration bands, multiple visual domains, and modality settings that include subtitles and audio.
papers.why_it_matters: You need Video-MME because many later model papers report it, but you should read it next to TempCompass so broad benchmark success does not get mistaken for temporal validity.
key_points: Covers broad video analysis rather than one narrowly temporal task.
Includes duration and modality variation, which makes it useful for realistic comparison.
Best used as a field-standard baseline before narrowing down to stricter temporal benchmarks.
next_after_summary: Compare it directly against TempCompass and ask what each benchmark forces the model to use.
Check whether a reported gain depends heavily on subtitles, audio, or long-context handling.
Use it as the broad baseline before moving to temporal hallucination or temporal validity benchmarks.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: MVBench: A Comprehensive Multi-modal Video Understanding Benchmark
papers.quick_takeaway: A broad benchmark that makes it harder for video models to hide behind one easy task type.
papers.problem: Single-task or narrow benchmarks can overstate how well a model understands time-sensitive video content.
papers.method_or_setup: Covers a wide range of video understanding tasks so failures are visible across more than one setting.
papers.why_it_matters: It gives a wider baseline for judging whether a temporal improvement is real or just benchmark-specific.
key_points: Broader task coverage exposes more failure modes.
Useful as a wide-angle comparison point.
Helps separate general video skill from temporal skill.
next_after_summary: Compare it with TempCompass and VidHalluc.
Check which task types break first.
Use it to sanity-check claims of general video understanding.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: TimeChat: A Time-sensitive Multimodal Large Language Model for Long Video Understanding
papers.quick_takeaway: A method paper that pushes the model to care about timestamps and long-video timing.
papers.problem: Long videos need explicit time binding, not just semantic summary ability.
papers.method_or_setup: Builds a time-sensitive multimodal model with stronger temporal awareness for long video understanding.
papers.why_it_matters: It is a clean anchor for asking whether timestamp awareness actually improves temporal faithfulness.
key_points: Focuses on explicit time sensitivity.
Targets long-video understanding rather than short clip QA.
Useful when comparing timestamp-aware and timestamp-blind models.
next_after_summary: Compare against MovieChat and LongVideoBench.
Ask whether time sensitivity helps actual grounding or just response style.
Use it as a method-side anchor for temporal localization.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: MovieChat: From Dense Token to Sparse Memory for Long Video Understanding
papers.quick_takeaway: A long-video memory paper that tries to compress dense video evidence into something a model can actually use.
papers.problem: Long videos are too dense for naive token-by-token reasoning.
papers.method_or_setup: Moves from dense tokens toward sparse memory so the model can track important information over long videos.
papers.why_it_matters: It shows how memory design affects whether temporal evidence survives long-context compression.
key_points: Dense video tokens are hard to scale.
Sparse memory is the core design move.
Relevant for long-context video reasoning, not only QA.
next_after_summary: Compare against TimeChat and LongVideoBench.
Check whether memory helps with order or just summarization.
Use it as the memory-design reference in the long-video cluster.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: MLVU: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Multi-Task Long Video Understanding
papers.quick_takeaway: A multi-task benchmark for long videos that helps test whether models degrade as context and task variety increase.
papers.problem: Long-video evaluation needs more than one task if we want to see context-related failure.
papers.method_or_setup: Evaluates multiple long-video understanding tasks in one benchmark.
papers.why_it_matters: It is a useful baseline for seeing whether a model's temporal strength holds across different tasks.
key_points: Multi-task setup makes evaluation broader.
Good for spotting context-length degradation.
Helps compare long-video reasoning against short-clip QA.
next_after_summary: Compare with LongVideoBench for long-context coverage.
Look for task diversity effects.
Use it to detect whether performance drops with broader task demands.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: LongVideoBench: A Benchmark for Long-context Interleaved Video-Language Understanding
papers.quick_takeaway: A benchmark for long-context interleaved video-language reasoning where retrieval and attention to scattered evidence matter.
papers.problem: Long-context video-language understanding is fragile when evidence is spread across time.
papers.method_or_setup: Builds a long-context benchmark with interleaved video-language inputs to stress temporal reasoning.
papers.why_it_matters: It helps test whether models can keep evidence aligned over longer mixed-modality inputs.
key_points: Interleaved inputs increase reasoning difficulty.
Long-context evidence is easy to lose.
Useful for studying retrieval-like behavior in video LLMs.
next_after_summary: Compare against MLVU and MovieChat.
Check whether retrieval quality predicts answers.
Use it when reasoning across multiple moments matters.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: Temporal Reasoning Transfer from Text to Video
papers.quick_takeaway: TRTV argues that video temporal reasoning fails not only because of visual encoding, but because the underlying LLM itself struggles with temporal concepts.
papers.problem: The bottleneck may be temporal reasoning in the language backbone, not just missing visual evidence.
papers.method_or_setup: Introduces T3, a text-only transfer setup that synthesizes temporal reasoning tasks from image-text datasets.
papers.why_it_matters: If the LLM backbone is weak on temporal concepts, the model can sound fluent while still hallucinating order, duration, or temporal relation facts.
key_points: A probing classifier suggests temporal information already exists in video representations.
T3 strengthens the LLM side without using video data.
Reported gains appear on TempCompass and Video-MME temporal reasoning.
next_after_summary: Use this paper when defining whether the bottleneck is perception or reasoning.
Test how far text-only transfer can go on harder grounded evidence tasks.
Compare with video-side fixes to see which lever matters most.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: Re-thinking Temporal Search for Long-Form Video Understanding
papers.quick_takeaway: A strong reframe of long-video understanding as finding the right sparse moments first.
papers.problem: Long-form video fails often because the model does not search the right evidence moments.
papers.method_or_setup: Treats long-video understanding as temporal search over sparse evidence rather than dense scanning.
papers.why_it_matters: It is one of the clearest papers for arguing that search quality is a bottleneck before reasoning quality.
key_points: Evidence is sparse in long videos.
Search should be judged separately from final answers.
Strong fit for temporal hallucination and retrieval failures.
next_after_summary: Compare with VITED and LongVideoBench.
Ask whether better search fixes hallucination.
Use it as the main long-video search reference.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: VITED: Video Temporal Evidence Distillation
papers.quick_takeaway: A paper that makes temporal evidence itself the object of learning.
papers.problem: Video models often answer without a clear evidence chain for the relevant time span.
papers.method_or_setup: Distills temporal evidence so long-form reasoning can be tied to more explicit video support.
papers.why_it_matters: It is useful for thinking about faithfulness, not just accuracy.
key_points: Evidence chains are central.
Targets long-form reasoning settings.
Good comparison point for search-first methods.
next_after_summary: Compare with lvhaystack and Consistency.
Ask whether evidence distillation improves faithfulness.
Use it when you want grounded reasoning rather than stylistic answers.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: On the Consistency of Video Large Language Models in Temporal Comprehension
papers.quick_takeaway: The paper shows that even when Video LLMs can find a moment once, they often cannot stay consistent when probed again.
papers.problem: A single correct timestamp is not enough if the model cannot preserve that belief across follow-up checks.
papers.method_or_setup: Starts from an initial moment prediction and applies grounding and verification probes to test self-consistency.
papers.why_it_matters: Temporal hallucination is often instability over repeated questioning, not just one wrong answer.
key_points: Separates grounding consistency from verification consistency.
Many models are sensitive to wording, content shifts, and task setup.
Event temporal verification tuning improves both grounding and consistency.
next_after_summary: Use this when arguing that temporal faithfulness needs repeated self-checks.
Compare with hallucination benchmarks to see whether errors are stable or just one-off.
Ask whether better perception or better verification is the real bottleneck.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: Omnia de EgoTempo: Benchmarking Temporal Understanding of Multi-Modal LLMs in Egocentric Videos
papers.quick_takeaway: A benchmark focused on egocentric video, where temporal understanding is often harder and shortcuts are easier to miss.
papers.problem: Egocentric videos create a different temporal difficulty profile than standard third-person video.
papers.method_or_setup: Benchmarks temporal understanding on egocentric video settings.
papers.why_it_matters: It is useful for exposing shortcutting in real-world first-person scenes.
key_points: Egocentric viewpoint changes the problem.
Good for temporal order and action understanding.
Helps test generalization beyond curated benchmark clips.
next_after_summary: Compare with TempCompass and VidHalluc.
Check whether egocentric scenes amplify order confusion.
Use it when you care about first-person temporal understanding.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: Seq2Time: Sequential Knowledge Transfer for Video LLM Temporal Grounding
papers.quick_takeaway: A transfer-style paper that tries to move sequential knowledge into temporal grounding.
papers.problem: Temporal grounding is hard when models do not transfer sequence knowledge well.
papers.method_or_setup: Uses sequential knowledge transfer to help video LLMs learn temporal grounding.
papers.why_it_matters: It is useful for asking whether grounding improves from data transfer or from new model structure.
key_points: Focuses on knowledge transfer.
Targets temporal grounding directly.
Relevant for data-centric improvement strategies.
next_after_summary: Compare against UniVTG and ProTeGe.
Ask whether transfer changes faithfulness or only benchmark score.
Use it when studying how temporal knowledge enters the model.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: STEP: Enhancing Video-LLMs' Compositional Reasoning by Spatio-Temporal Graph-guided Self-Training
papers.quick_takeaway: A compositional reasoning paper that uses graph-guided self-training to make spatio-temporal reasoning more explicit.
papers.problem: Video LLMs struggle when a question requires multiple linked temporal facts.
papers.method_or_setup: Adds spatio-temporal graph guidance and self-training to strengthen compositional reasoning.
papers.why_it_matters: It helps test whether explicit structure reduces temporal hallucination in multi-step reasoning.
key_points: Graph guidance makes reasoning more structured.
Self-training is part of the learning loop.
Good fit for compositional temporal questions.
next_after_summary: Compare with VideoComp and VITED.
Check whether structure helps faithfulness or only answer format.
Use it for compositional reasoning comparisons.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: VideoComp: Advancing Fine-Grained Compositional and Temporal Alignment in Video-Text Models
papers.quick_takeaway: A fine-grained alignment paper aimed at the subtle temporal mismatches that broad benchmarks often miss.
papers.problem: Models can look good on broad metrics while still failing on reordering and subtle negative examples.
papers.method_or_setup: Builds fine-grained compositional and temporal alignment signals for video-text models.
papers.why_it_matters: It is useful for catching the small temporal errors that become big hallucinations in practice.
key_points: Targets subtle negative pairs.
Focuses on alignment, not just classification.
Good stress test for temporal ordering.
next_after_summary: Compare with STEP and TempCompass.
Ask whether alignment gains transfer to free-form QA.
Use it to probe reordering sensitivity.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: Motion-Grounded Video Reasoning: Understanding and Perceiving Motion at Pixel Level
papers.quick_takeaway: A motion-grounded reasoning paper that pushes video understanding closer to pixel-level movement evidence.
papers.problem: Temporal reasoning is weak when the model ignores actual motion evidence.
papers.method_or_setup: Focuses on motion-grounded reasoning at the pixel level.
papers.why_it_matters: It is helpful when you want evidence grounded in visual change, not just scene semantics.
key_points: Motion is treated as first-class evidence.
Pixel-level grounding matters for temporal questions.
Relevant for causal and counterfactual reasoning.
next_after_summary: Compare with VITED and STEP.
Check whether motion grounding reduces hallucination.
Use it for evidence-centric temporal reasoning discussions.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection
papers.quick_takeaway: Good paper for thinking about core-frame selection and reasoning supervision.
papers.problem: Compositional temporal reasoning is often weak even when benchmark scores improve.
papers.method_or_setup: Focuses on explicit structure, evidence chains, or multi-step temporal reasoning.
papers.why_it_matters: Useful for checking whether the model can maintain grounded multi-step temporal reasoning.
key_points: Main focus: reasoning supervision.
Category: causal with CVPR 2025 context.
Best used as a B-tier reference for good paper for thinking about core-frame selection and reasoning supervision.
next_after_summary: Check whether the reasoning chain is grounded.
Compare with a benchmark paper to avoid score-only reading.
Ask whether the structure actually improves faithfulness.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: UniVTG: Towards Unified Video-Language Temporal Grounding
papers.quick_takeaway: A unified grounding view that ties moment retrieval, highlight detection, and browsing-style understanding together.
papers.problem: Temporal grounding tasks are often treated as separate problems even when they depend on the same underlying moment localization skill.
papers.method_or_setup: Presents a unified video-language temporal grounding framework for different query and localization settings.
papers.why_it_matters: It gives a clean baseline for seeing whether a model can actually find moments rather than just answer about them.
key_points: Unifies multiple grounding-style tasks.
Useful for comparing retrieval and detection behavior.
Anchors the older temporal grounding literature.
next_after_summary: Compare with ProTeGe and Guidance to see how training and search differ.
Check whether retrieval and highlight detection fail for the same reasons.
Use it as a grounding baseline when discussing temporal faithfulness.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: ProTeGe: Untrimmed Pretraining for Video Temporal Grounding by Video Temporal Grounding
papers.quick_takeaway: ProTeGe argues that pretraining on untrimmed videos can make boundary localization sharper.
papers.problem: Standard video pretraining does not necessarily teach the model where an event starts and ends.
papers.method_or_setup: Uses untrimmed video pretraining with a grounding-oriented objective to improve temporal boundary sensitivity.
papers.why_it_matters: If boundaries are weak, later temporal claims can look fluent while still being off in time.
key_points: Focuses on untrimmed pretraining.
Targets boundary sensitivity directly.
Useful as an older grounding anchor.
next_after_summary: Compare with UniVTG to separate architecture from pretraining effects.
Check whether better boundaries help hallucination resistance.
Use it when arguing for data-centric temporal grounding.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: Localizing Moments in Long Video Via Multimodal Guidance
papers.quick_takeaway: The paper uses multimodal guidance to prune long videos toward the relevant moment.
papers.problem: Long videos are too dense to search naively, so the model needs a way to narrow down the evidence.
papers.method_or_setup: Adds guidance signals that help localize moments in long videos by focusing the search space.
papers.why_it_matters: This is a practical reminder that temporal understanding often fails at retrieval before reasoning even starts.
key_points: Guided pruning is the main idea.
Long-video search is the bottleneck.
Useful comparator for sparse-evidence methods.
next_after_summary: Compare with ScanOnce and lvhaystack on search efficiency.
Ask whether pruning hurts recall of the right moment.
Use it to frame long-video grounding as evidence selection.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: Scanning Only Once: An End-to-end Framework for Fast Temporal Grounding in Long Videos
papers.quick_takeaway: ScanOnce pushes long-video grounding toward a one-pass, efficiency-first setup.
papers.problem: Repeated scanning over long videos is expensive and can amplify noisy search behavior.
papers.method_or_setup: Builds an end-to-end framework that scans the video only once for temporal grounding.
papers.why_it_matters: It is a strong reminder that speed and faithfulness can trade off in long-video understanding.
key_points: Efficiency is the core objective.
One-pass inference changes the search tradeoff.
Useful long-video baseline.
next_after_summary: Compare with Guidance and lvhaystack on search strategy.
Check whether faster grounding loses hard evidence.
Use it when discussing long-video efficiency constraints.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: Compositional Video Understanding with Spatiotemporal Structure-based Transformers
papers.quick_takeaway: This is a structure-first paper for compositional video understanding.
papers.problem: Compositional video reasoning breaks when models do not preserve spatial and temporal structure cleanly.
papers.method_or_setup: Uses a spatiotemporal structure-based transformer to improve compositional reasoning over video.
papers.why_it_matters: Structure can reduce shortcutting when the question depends on event order and object-state composition.
key_points: Explicit structure is the main bias.
Useful for compositional generalization.
Good comparison point for later hallucination mitigation papers.
next_after_summary: Compare with MASH-VLM and STEP on architectural bias.
Check whether structure helps order-sensitive failures.
Use it as a selective comparison paper, not a primary anchor.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: Benchmarking the Robustness of Temporal Action Detection Models Against Temporal Corruptions
papers.quick_takeaway: TADRobust turns temporal corruptions into a robustness benchmark for action detection.
papers.problem: Temporal action detectors can look strong until the evidence is corrupted or shifted.
papers.method_or_setup: Applies temporal corruptions and measures how action detection models degrade under them.
papers.why_it_matters: Robustness under corruption is a useful lens for deciding whether a model truly uses temporal evidence.
key_points: Focuses on temporal corruption stress tests.
Measures degradation, not just accuracy.
Good bridge to faithfulness thinking.
next_after_summary: Borrow corruption ideas for video LLM evaluation.
Compare robustness with benchmark-only performance.
Use it when discussing missing or noisy temporal evidence.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: VideoRefer Suite: Advancing Spatial-Temporal Object Understanding with Video LLM
papers.quick_takeaway: VideoRefer focuses on object-centric spatial-temporal understanding in video LLMs.
papers.problem: A lot of temporal error starts with wrong object-state tracking, not just wrong event order.
papers.method_or_setup: Provides a suite for spatial-temporal object understanding in video-language settings.
papers.why_it_matters: Object-centric tracking is a useful subproblem when you want to diagnose why temporal hallucination happens.
key_points: Object-centric rather than pure event-centric.
Covers spatial-temporal relations.
Selective comparison paper for faithfulness.
next_after_summary: Compare with grounding and hallucination benchmarks.
Check whether object tracking improves temporal consistency.
Use it when the failure looks like state tracking, not just order confusion.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: Towards Debiasing Temporal Sentence Grounding in Video
papers.quick_takeaway: This is an early warning that dataset bias can make temporal grounding look better than it really is.
papers.problem: Annotation and dataset biases can let models exploit shortcuts instead of grounding the right moment.
papers.method_or_setup: Studies and mitigates bias sources in temporal sentence grounding datasets and setups.
papers.why_it_matters: It helps explain why benchmark progress may not reflect true temporal understanding.
key_points: Bias diagnosis is the main contribution.
Important pathology anchor for grounding.
Useful when benchmark gains look too easy.
next_after_summary: Compare with TempCompass on shortcut detection.
Use it when discussing dataset bias and overfitting.
Check whether your favorite benchmark is bias-sensitive.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: VidHalluc: Evaluating Temporal Hallucinations in Multimodal Large Language Models for Video Understanding
papers.quick_takeaway: A benchmark that turns temporal hallucination into a measurable failure mode across action, sequence, and scene transition.
papers.problem: The field needs to measure video hallucination directly, not only overall answer accuracy.
papers.method_or_setup: Builds 5,002 paired videos across three hallucination dimensions and evaluates whether models stay faithful to video evidence.
papers.why_it_matters: It is one of the most direct references for discussing temporal hallucination as a first-class evaluation target.
key_points: Pairs are designed to be hallucination-prone.
Evaluation covers action, temporal sequence, and scene transition.
DINO-HEAL offers a training-free mitigation path.
next_after_summary: Use it as the main benchmark when you want hallucination metrics.
Check whether benchmark gains transfer to open-ended video understanding.
Compare with TempCompass and Consistency to separate different temporal failure types.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: Temporal Sentence Grounding in Videos: A Survey and Future Directions
papers.quick_takeaway: A survey anchor that gives the vocabulary and taxonomy for temporal sentence grounding.
papers.problem: The temporal grounding literature is large, but the task names and definitions are fragmented.
papers.method_or_setup: Summarizes the temporal sentence grounding field and its future directions.
papers.why_it_matters: Useful for framing, naming, and comparing older grounding papers without getting lost in terminology.
key_points: Best for terminology and taxonomy.
Historical anchor rather than a method paper.
Useful for related-work framing.
next_after_summary: Use it to orient newer grounding papers.
Do not treat it as core evidence for hallucination claims.
Mine it for task definitions and evaluation terms.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: A Survey of Video Datasets for Grounded Event Understanding
papers.quick_takeaway: This survey maps the dataset landscape for grounded event understanding.
papers.problem: Event-centric video datasets are scattered across tasks, making it hard to know what is actually being measured.
papers.method_or_setup: Reviews video datasets and task framing for grounded event understanding.
papers.why_it_matters: It helps you separate true temporal reasoning from dataset-specific framing choices.
key_points: Dataset landscape is the main focus.
Grounded events are broader than single-span grounding.
Useful survey anchor for task selection.
next_after_summary: Use it when picking evaluation datasets.
Compare with TSGV survey for terminology and scope.
Check where temporal hallucination is actually measured.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.

papers.title: MASH-VLM: Mitigating Action-Scene Hallucination in Video-LLMs through Disentangled Spatial-Temporal Representations
papers.quick_takeaway: MASH-VLM argues that action-scene hallucination comes from entangled spatial-temporal features and positional bias, then attacks both directly.
papers.problem: Video models can infer the wrong action from scene context or infer the wrong scene from an observed action.
papers.method_or_setup: Uses DST-attention plus Harmonic-RoPE to separate spatial and temporal signals more cleanly.
papers.why_it_matters: Many temporal hallucinations are entanglement errors, where scene cues leak into event prediction.
key_points: DST-attention masks spatial and temporal tokens separately.
Harmonic-RoPE reduces positional bias across token types.
UNSCENE is the main evaluation benchmark.
next_after_summary: Compare it against benchmark-first papers like TempCompass and VidHalluc.
Check whether disentangling helps longer multi-event reasoning.
Use it as the main architecture paper for hallucination mitigation.
papers.figure_caption: Paper first-page preview for quick scanning.
